coalition_analyses.expected_friction: Binding constraint is regulation (0.4) --- FDA/EMA trial pipelines and LMIC regulatory harmonization are the rate-limiter, not compute or capex. Suffering scores look directionally right: disease (0.85) and mortality (0.7) are the layers this actually touches; the 0.3 on poverty is generous --- drug discovery reduces DALYs but doesn't move income, so credit the mortality/disease channel and stop double-counting. Harm scores are plausibly overstated on concentration (0.7) --- therapeutic IP concentrates heavily in a handful of pharma incumbents and the AI layer on top tends to entrench them further; the real harm layer the scores miss is access-pricing lock-in, not water or land.

Binding constraint is regulation (0.45) plus enterprise absorption (0.55) --- clinical liability, licensure across jurisdictions, and payer integration are what stall deployment, not the model. Suffering numerator is mechanism-honest: mental_health (0.7) is the layer it touches; the 0.3 on mortality is defensible via suicide-prevention pathway but thin. Harm scores miss the real risk: iatrogenic harm from LLM-mediated crisis response is a catastrophic-tail single-layer failure that averaging near-1 water/land scores washes out. Concentration (0.5) is also understated --- mental health data captured by a frontier lab is among the most sensitive possible training substrates.

Friction is low across the board and the scores reflect that --- this is a fund-the-work intervention with no grid, regulatory, or public blocker of note. The suffering numerator is correctly near-zero on the direct layers; alignment's role is not to reduce suffering itself but to protect the denominator against catastrophic downside. The net_composite of 0.067 therefore under-prices it relative to its enabling function: every high-suffering-reduction intervention downstream is conditional on alignment not failing. Harm scores look right. The real mispricing is structural --- ranking by direct suffering delta punishes interventions whose value is insurance against tail risk.

Binding constraint is grid (0.4) --- interconnection queues and generation timelines are the real ceiling, not chips or capex. Suffering scores are a category error: compute doesn't reduce suffering, it enables interventions that might; crediting 0.4 on disease and 0.3 on mental_health smuggles downstream effects into an enabling layer. Harm scores are badly under-counted --- water (0.2), land (0.5), concentration (0.2), and extraction (0.3) are all first-order costs of datacenter buildout that the mean-harm aggregator averages into a 0.35 robustness, which is the lowest on the board but still understates how concentrated the harm is. The harm_robustness number is doing real work here and roughly matches reality.

Binding constraint is public acceptance (0.35) --- cultivated meat state-level bans and consumer resistance are the blocker, not the science. Suffering numerator is mechanism-honest: animal (0.85) is exactly the layer this touches and the weight is defensible given factory-farm scale. Harm scores look roughly right but under-count displacement (0.7) --- rural agricultural economies absorb the full cost of protein-substrate replacement and the coalition graph shows no camp actively representing that. The coalition is also thin --- only animal_welfare holds this as a first-priority normative claim, so mobilization is structurally weak even when the EV is strong.

Binding constraint is regulation (0.3) --- NEPA, state PUC processes, and interconnection queues are the actual wall. Suffering scores are another enabling-layer category error: grid buildout doesn't directly reduce disease, mortality, or poverty; it enables everything downstream. The 0.3 mortality and 0.3 disease scores are crediting second-order effects. Harm scores under-count land (0.3) and concentration (0.7 is too generous given utility-scale capture dynamics) --- transmission siting is where environmental-justice coalitions actually bind and the score doesn't reflect that.

Binding constraint is regulation (0.7 --- relatively open) and enterprise absorption (0.5) --- employer demand for retrained workers is the real ceiling, not program capacity. Suffering numerator is weak and the scores reflect it honestly: retraining touches poverty (0.3) and mental_health (0.3) at the margin but is fundamentally a mitigation intervention for harms caused by other interventions, not a primary suffering-reduction lever. Harm scores are near-perfect and probably correct --- this is the lowest-harm intervention on the board. Low net_composite is accurate; the intervention's real function is political cover for everything else on the list.

contested_claims.claim_text: DoD obligated AI-related contract spending rose substantially 2022-2025, driven by JWCC, Project Maven, and CDAO-managed pilots; precise totals are hampered by inconsistent AI tagging on contract line items.

contradicts.locator: Investigative pieces on DoD AI pilot failures and miscategorization
contradicts.source_title: The Intercept coverage of Palantir contracts and DoD AI programs

supports.locator: AI funding appendix; DoD budget rollups
supports.source_title: Artificial Intelligence and National Security (CRS Report R45178)

contested_claims.claim_text: No other pure-play US defense-AI software vendor has matched Palantir's contract backlog or combatant-command integration depth; cloud-provider primes (AWS, Microsoft, Google, Oracle via JWCC) supply infrastructure, not mission-software integration.

contradicts.locator: Coverage framing Palantir as over-sold relative to internal-tool alternatives
contradicts.source_title: The Intercept coverage of Palantir contracts and DoD AI programs

Palantir Technologies Inc. Form 10-K Annual Report (FY 2024)

contested_claims.claim_text: Credible 2030 forecasts for US datacenter share of electricity consumption diverge by more than 2x --- from ~4.6% (IEA/EPRI conservative) to ~9% (Goldman Sachs, EPRI high scenario) --- reflecting genuine uncertainty, not measurement error.

contradicts.source_title: Generational growth: AI, data centers and the coming US power demand surge

qualifies.locator: Analysing Electricity Demand; data centres chapter

supports.source_title: Powering Intelligence: Analyzing Artificial Intelligence and Data Center Energy Consumption

contested_claims.claim_text: Frontier-lab and big-tech employees have episodically resisted DoD contracts (Google Maven 2018, Microsoft IVAS 2019, Microsoft/OpenAI IDF deployments 2024), producing temporary pauses but no sustained shift in vendor willingness.

contradicts.locator: Karp interviews dismissing employee resistance as inconsequential
contradicts.source_title: Alex Karp public interviews and op-eds, 2023-2024

supports.source_title: Google employee open letter opposing Project Maven
Microsoft employee open letter opposing HoloLens/IVAS contract
Coverage of OpenAI and Microsoft AI use by Israeli military, 2024

ranking_blindspots.reasoning: Ranked #3 by direct suffering delta but its load-bearing function is as an enabling precondition for every downstream intervention; a composite that scores only direct suffering reduction systematically under-prices insurance against catastrophic tail risk.

Suffering_reduction_scores credit compute for disease (0.4), mental_health (0.3), and mortality (0.4) layers it does not directly touch --- these are downstream effects of interventions compute enables, smuggled into the enabling layer's numerator and inflating its suffering_composite.

Same enabling-layer error as compute --- grid buildout is credited for disease and mortality suffering reduction it doesn't mechanically cause, and harm_land (0.3) plus harm_concentration (0.7 is generous) under-count transmission-siting and utility-capture costs that are the actual binding harms.

mean(harm_robustness)=0.758 masks a catastrophic single-layer tail --- iatrogenic harm from LLM-mediated crisis response is not captured in the water/land/extraction axes that average the score upward, and no harm dimension in the current schema prices it.

harm_concentration (0.7) understates pharma IP entrenchment; the intervention's real access-pricing harm layer is absent from the harm schema entirely, so the top-ranked intervention is top-ranked partly because the relevant harm axis isn't measured.

Coalition support is structurally thin --- only camp_animal_welfare holds it as first-priority, and camp_displaced_workers opposes; net_composite doesn't reflect that mobilization capacity, not viability, is the binding constraint on interventions with narrow coalitions.

rankings.intervention_text: Deploy frontier AI (structure prediction, candidate screening, trial simulation) inside drug discovery and therapeutic development pipelines targeting neglected infectious disease, antimicrobial resistance, and LMIC-priority therapeutics.

Scale AI-assisted mental health triage, initial-line support, and care-navigation tooling targeted at the ~70% of global mental-health burden currently untreated, with integration into public health systems and evaluation against clinical outcomes.
Scale funding for interpretability and alignment research.
Expand frontier-lab compute capacity (chips, datacenters, networking).

Accelerate alternative-protein development (precision fermentation, cultivated meat, plant-based) with AI-driven strain engineering, scaffolding optimization, and supply-chain cost-down, targeting displacement of factory-farm protein at scale.
Accelerate grid and generation buildout (permitting reform, interconnection, new generation).
Invest in AI workforce training and retraining programs.
